title: Suminagashi: ink floating on water
subtitle: The Japanese practice of floating sumi ink on plain water to create concentric ring patterns in paper
excerpt: Suminagashi is a form of marbling in which drops of sumi ink and a surfactant ink alternate on the surface of still water, creating expanding concentric rings. Blowing gently moves the rings into irregular patterns. The technique requires no carrageenan size and no alum mordanting.
sourceNotes: Charles W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881), appendix on Japanese ink-on-water techniques. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/artofmarbling00wool. Woolnough contrasts the Japanese plain-water floating method with the carrageenan-size Western tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: Literally 'ink floating'. The Japanese art of floating sumi ink on plain water to create concentric ring and flowing patterns transferred to paper. Developed in Japan from the Heian period (794-1185). Unlike Western marbling, no thickened size is required and no alum treatment is needed.

The characteristic pattern of suminagashi: alternating rings of dark sumi ink and surfactant-loaded clear ink expanding outward from each drop contact point. Each subsequent drop pushes the previous rings outward.

content.text: differs from carrageenan marbling in almost every particular. The water is plain and still, not thickened with seaweed extract. The inks are water-based sumi ink alternating with a surfactant-loaded ink rather than acrylic pigments. The paper needs no alum mordanting. The patterns are
and organic flows rather than combed geometric designs. The two techniques share only the basic principle of floating pigment on water and lifting it onto paper.

A suminagashi ink set contains at least two inks: one standard dark sumi ink and one sumi ink loaded with a surfactant. The surfactant ink is the key: it spreads away from the dark ink, pushing it outward to form a ring. Alternate drops of dark and surfactant ink create expanding rings that interleave with each other. Breath or a gentle fan movement across the surface distorts the rings into flowing shapes.

You also need plain tap water at room temperature. Use thin paper at 50 to 100 gsm: newsprint, cartridge paper, or thin kozo all work. You also need a brush or dropper bottle for placing ink drops, and a rack or flat surface to dry the finished sheets on.
Fill the marbling tray with plain water to a depth of approximately 3 cm. Allow to settle for one minute until completely still.

Touch the tip of the dark sumi ink brush very gently to the water surface at the centre of the tray. The ink should spread outward to form a circle 3 to 5 cm across. If it sinks, the ink is too thick: dilute it slightly with water.

Touch the surfactant ink brush to the centre of the dark ink circle. The surfactant ink repels the dark ink outward, forming a ring. The pattern now has an inner ring of surfactant ink and an outer ring of dark ink.
Alternate drops of dark and surfactant ink, each placed at the centre of the previous drop. Build up six to twelve rings. Do not over-drop: the rings will crowd together and lose definition.

Optionally, blow across the surface gently or hold a thin strip of paper vertically and trail it through the pattern to introduce movement. Each breath or touch is irreversible: if you dislike the result, skim the surface and start again.

Lay the paper flat onto the surface for three to five seconds, then lift from one corner. The pattern transfers immediately. Lay the print flat to dry. The water on the paper surface carries a very thin film of ink and will dry without rinsing, unlike carrageenan marbling.

Ink sinks rather than floating: the ink is too thick or too old. Dilute further or use fresh ink. Rings do not form: the surfactant ink has been diluted too much during storage. Shake the bottle before use. Paper tears on lifting: the paper is too heavy or wet; use a lighter sheet. Pattern is too faint: the dark ink is too dilute. Both inks must be at full working strength.
